Sustainable Smart Bus Shelter — CUSD / TCAT
Cornell Systems Engineering · CUSD / TCAT
Resume
MBSE · Physical Systems · SYSEN

A bus shelter.
Modelled as a
safety-critical system.

TCAT bus stops in Ithaca are exposed infrastructure with no emergency capability, no real-time information, and grid-dependent power. This project applied 32 systems engineering tools across the complete development lifecycle, from stakeholder affinity mapping through SysML modelling, subsystem allocation, risk analysis, and verification planning, to design a solar-powered smart shelter with active emergency response.

32SE tools applied
36Requirements (OR+DR)
2.2kWSolar · 72hr autonomy
6Subsystems
01 · Problem

Exposed transit stops need engineered safety

TCAT shelters lack emergency capability, real-time information, and resilient power, modelled here as a safety-critical physical system.

What we are building

A solar-powered smart bus shelter for TCAT (Tompkins Consolidated Area Transit) in Ithaca, NY. Beyond weather protection, it functions as an active safety system, emergency communication with GPS and two-way audio, motion-activated LED lighting at 140–200 lux, real-time bus arri…

Why MBSE

Model-Based Systems Engineering replaces document-centric development with linked models, requirements connected to architecture, architecture connected to verification, verification connected to test procedures. For the shelter this meant: stakeholder needs → affinity clusters …

Technical depth

Stakeholders, requirements, architecture, and V&V

Full bus-shelter MBSE documentation preserved below, including all figures and tool-chain tables.

01

Project Background & SE Approach

This project designs a sustainable smart bus shelter for TCAT in Ithaca, NY, in collaboration with Cornell University Sustainable Design (CUSD) and Prof. Sirietta Simoncini. The methodology blends design-thinking empathy work with structured systems engineering, fieldwork and user engagement alongside formal modelling tools.

32 SE tools were applied across the complete project lifecycle. Each tool's output fed the next; no isolated deliverables. The tool chain below shows the sequence and what each tool enabled.

01
CVP
Why TCAT should choose this shelter, competitive comparison against EcoStop (B) and SEEDiA (C), defining non-negotiable features: energy autonomy, emergency communication, real-time info
→ QFD, requirements
02
Concept sketches
Structural and functional annotated sketches, spatial design, subsystem placement, passenger flow, system boundary definition
→ use cases, affinity
03
Customer Affinity Process
Stakeholder inputs clustered into themes: Sustainability, Safety & Security, Smart Tech, Accessibility, Maintenance/Operations
→ requirements, use cases
04–06
Context + Use Cases + SysML UCD
32 use cases with priorities; SysML diagram with include/extend logic; system boundary formalized; confirmation criteria defined
→ requirements, activity models
09–10
OR/DR Requirements + SysML Req Diag
30 originating requirements + 6 derived from ODT; SysML requirement blocks in Innoslate; all requirements traceable to stakeholder needs
→ architecture, VCRM
13–14
FFBD + IDEF0
Functional flow for emergency response: 18 functions F.1.1–F.1.18; IDEF0 A0 top-level + A1 decomposition with inputs/controls/outputs/mechanisms
→ subsystem allocation
15–20
Decision Matrix + GQM + AHP + QFD
Sustainability and structural durability weighted 0.22 each (AHP); QFD translated to engineering specs: 2.2kW solar, 150–200 lux, IP65 rating, 60 lbs/sq ft snow load
→ subsystem definition
19–21
Subsystem Definition + ODT + State Diagram
6 subsystems defined; operational states: Standby → Active/Occupied → Active Info Mode → Emergency Mode; state transitions specified
→ interface matrix, sequence
22–23
Interface Matrix + Sequence Diagram
Lighting subsystem discovered to need 40% more mounting space; sequence across all 6 subsystems for normal and emergency use
→ test plans
24–26
Test Plans + VCRM
Behavioral test plan (TP.1–TP.6) + non-behavioral test methodologies (TP.7–TP.10); VCRM confirms all 36 requirements have verification coverage
→ risk analysis
27–31
Severity + RPN + FMEA + Event Tree + Parametric
Emergency communication RPN 16 → mitigated to low criticality; event tree from emergency switch press to 8 outcome branches; parametric model exposed 38-hour battery failure
→ design refinement
02

Stakeholder Analysis & System Context

Eight external actors interact with the Smart Bus Shelter. The affinity process collected and clustered 20+ stakeholder questions into five thematic groups. These clusters directly informed the originating requirements.

03

Use Cases & Requirements

32 use cases defined with priority ratings and confirmation criteria. 30 originating requirements (OR1–OR30) covering accessibility, weather protection, communication, energy, structural resilience, regulatory compliance, and real-time information. 6 derived requirements (DR31–DR36) from subsystem allocation. Subsystem-specific obligations that trace back to higher-level originating requirements.

Requirements insight: The confirmation criteria in the user story drove precision that requirements alone couldn't achieve, "passengers get route/arrival info in <30 sec," "emergency alert connects within 10 sec," "solar + battery sustain operations 48 hrs." These became the testable acceptance criteria that shaped every downstream design decision.
04

System Architecture & Subsystem Allocation

Structural System
Canopy, frame, seating, accessibility clearances, load-bearing for 60 lbs/sq ft snow, solar panel mounts. Foundation for every other subsystem.
Power & Energy System
2.2kW PV panels, 10kWh battery storage, battery management, charge control. Generates min. 3 kWh/day, 85% transfer efficiency, 12-hr lighting autonomy.
Lighting System
LED fixtures, motion sensors, ambient light detection (50 lux threshold ±5 lux), automated control logic. 150–200 lux at 6000K. Responds within 500ms of detection.
Information Display
Real-time TCAT API integration, ETA display, route info, overcrowding alerts. Bus arrival info within 30 seconds. Readable in sunlight and dark.
Emergency Response
Panic button, GPS + shelter ID transmission within 5 sec, strobe activation within 1 sec, audio confirmation (3 beeps), two-way voice with dispatch within 10 sec.
Communication System
Cellular modem, TCAT API connection, police dispatch link, backup communication path, health monitoring data transmission, incident logging.

Interface analysis finding: The lighting subsystem required 40% more mounting space than initial allocation. Initial sketches showed four LED fixtures. Interface matrix analysis revealed dedicated housing for drivers, heat sinks, motion sensor integration points, and emergency backup circuits that wouldn't fit the original envelope. This is the kind of problem that surfaces formally through MBSE and silently on a construction site.

05

Behavioural Models: Emergency Response Focus

The emergency response scenario is the most time-critical and safety-critical use case. It was modelled at four levels of abstraction: use case behavioral table → activity diagram → FFBD → IDEF0. Each level revealed different aspects of the system's required behaviour.

What the FFBD revealed: Behind a single user action (pressing the emergency button) exists 18 tightly sequenced functions, many in parallel (strobe, audio, GPS transmission, camera activation all concurrent). Only by laying out these micro-interactions did hidden dependencies surface: communication delays could prevent GPS transmission, single-point failures in the cellular link would cascade to lost emergency response. This drove the redundancy requirements in the communication subsystem.
06

Risk Analysis & Verification

Emergency comm RPN initial16Severity 4 × Likelihood 4
After first mitigation8Monthly mockup checks
After full mitigationLowRedundant switch + bi-weekly checks
Requirements verified36/36VCRM full coverage

Event tree for emergency switch activation, 8 outcome branches from best (all systems work → timely assistance) to worst (detection fails → no response). Each branch combines probabilities P1–P6 multiplicatively. Analysis identified communication failure and power backup failure as the highest-consequence branches, driving the redundancy architecture in both subsystems.

FMEA key finding: emergency communication
Initial RPN = 16 (severity 4, likelihood 4). Corrective action sequence: (1) monthly mockup check with TCAT and police → RPN 8. Not acceptable for a safety-critical function. (2) Redundant switch mechanism + bi-weekly checks → RPN reduced to low criticality. The FMEA made this explicit. It would have been a field discovery otherwise.
VCRM: every requirement has a test
TP.1–TP.6: behavioral tests (emergency activation, accessibility, lighting response, information display, solar operation, weather protection). TP.7–TP.10: non-behavioral tests (solar output, structural load, carbon footprint, sensor accuracy). VCRM matrix confirmed all 36 requirements (OR1–OR30, DR31–DR36) mapped to at least one test procedure with no gaps.
Structural load test criteria
OR12: roof shall support 60 lbs/sq ft static snow load. OR21: structural deflection under that load shall not exceed 2cm. OR11: drainage shall evacuate rainwater at ≥ 6 inches/hour. All three tied to TP.8. Specific, measurable, testable, tracing directly from stakeholder concern ("Can this shelter hold up through Ithaca's snowstorms?") to quantified acceptance criterion.
Energy verification targets
OR15: generate ≥ 120% of average daily consumption (min 3 kWh/day). OR18: battery ≥ 10 kWh. OR23: power all lighting loads for ≥ 12 hours on battery only. OR28: solar-to-battery transfer efficiency ≥ 85%. All mapped to TP.7. Parametric analysis (Section 07) revealed the 72-hour autonomy target required re-evaluation against these criteria.
07

SysML Parametric Analysis: Power Budget

The parametric diagram quantifies power flows through the shelter. The most critical constraint in the system. Three constraint blocks: Total Power (TP = PS − PU), Power Stored (PS = SP + GP), Power Usage (PU = MC + BC + L).

The finding: running all loads simultaneously during worst-case Ithaca winter (peak heating demand, minimal solar generation, extended emergency mode) drains the battery in 38 hours, not the 72-hour target. The parametric model made this visible before physical build.

Design decision forced by parametric analysis: Three options considered, (1) Increase battery capacity by 35% (cost increase ~$800); (2) Implement non-critical load shedding during emergency mode (bike chargers and display brightness reduction : saves ~30% load); (3) Accept 48-hour autonomy target. Option 2 was selected as primary mitigation, with 48-hour autonomy as the revised operational requirement. This forced the OR18 battery requirement to be revised from 72h to 48h with non-critical shedding protocol.
08

Project Reflection

The shelter study ran 32 SE tools across the lifecycle: stakeholder work set requirements, requirements drove architecture and subsystem allocation, and those artifacts defined what would need to be verified on a deployable TCAT asset.

Three things I would not have found without the formal methods:

The 40% lighting space problem
Interface matrix analysis revealed the lighting subsystem needed significantly more structural mounting space than initial allocation. This kind of spatial conflict is invisible in requirements documents and use case diagrams. It only surfaces when you formally map what each subsystem requires from every other. In a real construction project, this would have been a site RFI with schedule and cost implications.
The 38-hour battery failure
The parametric model showed our initial solar sizing looked good on paper but failed under realistic Ithaca winter conditions. This forced us to either increase battery capacity by 35%, reduce non-critical loads, or accept a lower autonomy threshold. Without the quantitative model, we would have built to the 72-hour target and discovered the failure during cold-weather testing, much later, much more expensive.
MBSE becomes more useful when you already know what breaks in the field
8 years of EPC execution changes how you read a requirements document. Buildability and maintainability, "can a technician access this in January in Ithaca?", started mattering as much as theoretical correctness. The MBSE framework gave structure to that intuition: maintenance access requirements surfaced conflicts that drawings wouldn't catch because the tools forced you to model maintenance as a use case, not an afterthought.
Construction experience carried into MBSE
Cornell did not replace earlier site work. It added vocabulary and methods for patterns already visible on projects: interfaces matter, timing matters, and tradeoffs rarely stay local. Modelling the shelter on paper surfaced the lighting-space and battery-autonomy issues before fabrication.
